Euclid Surface Photometry Pipeline
  • Scientific Background

Scientific Background

Disc breaks in surface brightness profiles

Most of the baryonic mass and angular momentum of galaxies reside in the disc component, making the radial distribution of stellar light a key tracer of disc formation and evolution. It is now well established that the radial surface brightness profiles of disc galaxies frequently deviate from a single exponential, displaying changes in slope referred to as disc breaks (Pohlen and Trujillo [1]; Erwin et al. [2]).

Following the classification scheme of Pohlen and Trujillo [1] and Erwin et al. [2], disc galaxy profiles are divided into three categories:

Disc break types

Type

Nickname

Description

Type I

Pure exponential

Single exponential disc spanning several scale lengths with no break signature.

Type II

Down-bending

Broken exponential with a steeper outer slope (truncated disc). Dominant in the local Universe (~50 % of disc galaxies at \(z<0.1\)).

Type III

Up-bending

Broken exponential with a shallower outer slope (anti-truncated disc). Becomes progressively more common at higher redshift.

Composite profiles with two or more breaks, such as Type II+III, Type III+II, Type II+II, and Type III+III, are also identified by the pipeline (Module 5).

Physical origin

Type II breaks are most commonly associated with either star-formation thresholds linked to the gas surface density (Kennicutt [3]) or with radial stellar migration driven by bars and other secular processes (Debattista et al. [4]). Their brighter break surface brightness (~23.0 mag arcsec⁻²) and their prevalence at late cosmic times support a scenario in which dynamically settled, gas-depleted discs preferentially develop down-bending profiles.

Type III breaks arise from a wider variety of mechanisms including minor mergers, galaxy harassment, accretion of dark matter substructure, and the build-up of thick discs or stellar haloes (Borlaff et al. [5]; Roediger et al. [6]). Their characteristic fainter break level (~24.2 mag arcsec⁻²) and the increase of their frequency toward higher redshifts are consistent with external environmental effects being more dominant at earlier cosmic epochs.

Redshift evolution measured with Euclid Q1

Using the pipeline described in this documentation applied to 4 385 well-classified disc galaxies out to \(z=1\), Sánchez-Alarcón et al. (2026) find:

  • Type II profiles are the most common locally (>50 %) but decrease steadily with lookback time.

  • Type III and Type II+III fractions rise from ~10 % at \(z\approx0.05\) to ~30 % each at \(z\approx1\).

  • The break radius correlates tightly with the outer disc scale length: \(r_\mathrm{b}/h_\mathrm{out} \approx 2.9\), irrespective of break type.

Sample selection

Galaxies are drawn from the Euclid Q1 Merged catalogue (; Euclid Collaboration: Romelli et al. [7]) using the following criteria:

Sample selection criteria

Parameter

Label

Criterion

Remaining

smooth-or-featured_featured-or-disk_fraction

\(P_\mathrm{disc}\)

> 0.6

84 071

kron_radius

\(R_\mathrm{Kron}\)

> 11 arcsec

14 667

phz_pp_median_redshift

\(z\)

< 1

11 687

|Δz|/z_mean

\(\Delta z\)

< 0.01

8 748

The morphological disc probability \(P_\mathrm{disc}\) comes from the deep-learning catalogue of using the Zoobot foundation model (Walmsley et al. [8]).
